tool_input.prompt:
I'm planning a new "AI-generated custom quote system" for a Next.js 16 + LibSQL (Turso) app at /Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web. Please explore and report on the following areas:

1. **DB schema**: Find all schema definition files (SQL files, migration files, or TypeScript schema files). I need to know the current `quotes` table structure — what columns exist, what types are there.

2. **Quote system**: Find all files related to the current quote system — types, DB queries, server actions, and any existing components. Focus on: what quote types exist, how they're structured, what columns the current quote table has.

3. **Admin area**: Map the existing admin area structure. What routes exist under `/app/admin/`? What patterns do admin pages follow (Server Components, data fetching patterns, action handlers)?

4. **Email (Resend) setup**: Find where Resend is used for email sending. What's the email sending pattern? Are there existing email templates?

5. **LibSQL client setup**: How is the DB client initialized? What's the query pattern used (raw SQL vs ORM)?

Report file paths, key function names, and code snippets where relevant. Focus on patterns I should reuse — don't summarize things I don't need to know.

content.text:
Here is a complete report on all five areas: --- ## 1. DB Schema — `quotes` Table **Defined and auto-migrated in:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/shared/lib/db.ts` (`initDb()`) Full column list: | Column | Type | Notes | |---|---|---| | `id` | TEXT PK | nanoid(10) | | `client_name` | TEXT NOT NULL | | | `client_email` | TEXT NOT NULL | | | `client_company` | TEXT | default `''` | | `title` | TEXT NOT NULL | | | `description` | TEXT | default `''` | | `quote_type` | TEXT | `'line-items'` or `'blueprint'` | | `line_items` | TEXT | JSON array, default `'[]'` | | `currency` | TEXT | `USD`, `EUR`, `MXN`, `COP` | | `discount` | REAL | 0–100 percent | | `notes` | TEXT | | | `status` | TEXT | `draft`, `sent`, `accepted`, `rejected` | | `valid_until` | TEXT | ISO date string | | `locale` | TEXT | `'en'` or `'es'` | | `problem` | TEXT | blueprint only | | `opportunity` | TEXT | blueprint only | | `deliverables` | TEXT | JSON array of `{id, number, title, description}` | | `timeline` | TEXT | blueprint only | | `fixed_price` | REAL | blueprint only | | `list_price` | REAL | blueprint only | | `preferential_note` | TEXT | blueprint only | | `payment_terms` | TEXT | blueprint only | | `next_steps` | TEXT | JSON array of strings | | `scope_note` | TEXT | blueprint only | | `created_at` | TEXT | `datetime('now')` | | `updated_at` | TEXT | `datetime('now')` | There are no SQL migration files — migrations are inline `ALTER TABLE` statements in `initDb()`, run silently on startup with errors suppressed. --- ## 2. Quote System — Types, Queries, Actions, Components **Types:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/features/quotes/types/index.ts` Two quote types: `"line-items"` and `"blueprint"`. The `Quote` interface maps every DB column to camelCase. JSON columns (`line_items`, `deliverables`, `next_steps`) are parsed in the `rowToQuote` mapper. Blueprint fields are optional on the interface. **Zod schema:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/features/quotes/lib/schemas.ts` `quoteFormSchema` is the single source of truth for validation. Key sub-schemas: `lineItemSchema` and `deliverableSchema`. **DB queries:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/features/quotes/lib/queries.ts` Functions: `getAllQuotes(filters?)`, `getQuoteById(id)`, `createQuote(data)`, `updateQuote(id, data)`, `updateQuoteStatus(id, status)`, `deleteQuote(id)`. All use raw SQL with `db.execute({ sql, args })`. The `rowToQuote()` helper centralizes row-to-type mapping — add any new column mapping here. **Server actions:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/features/quotes/actions/index.ts` `"use server"` file. Functions: `createQuoteAction`, `updateQuoteAction`, `deleteQuoteAction`, `acceptQuoteAction`, `sendQuoteAction`. Pattern: parse FormData → `quoteFormSchema.safeParse()` → call query → `revalidatePath()`. The `sendQuoteAction` additionally calls `sendQuoteToClient()` from email lib and sets status to `"sent"`. **Components:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/features/quotes/components/` - `QuoteForm.tsx` — shared create/edit form (accepts optional `quote` prop) - `QuoteViewer.tsx` — public-facing quote view - `QuoteTable.tsx` — admin list table - `QuoteFilters.tsx` — search/status/type filter bar - `Pagination.tsx` — page controls --- ## 3. Admin Area Structure Routes under `[locale]/admin`: ``` /admin → page.tsx (dashboard, lists all quotes) /admin/contacts → contacts/page.tsx (contact submissions) /admin/quotes/new → quotes/new/page.tsx /admin/quotes/[id]/edit → quotes/[id]/edit/page.tsx ``` **Auth guard:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/app/[locale]/admin/layout.tsx` — async Server Component, calls `isAuthenticated()` from `@/features/auth/lib/auth`. If not authed, renders `` instead of children. No redirect, no middleware-level protection in the layout itself. **Page pattern — all admin pages are async Server Components:** - Await `getTranslations("Admin")` for i18n strings - `params` typed as `Promise<{...}>` and awaited (Next.js 16 pattern) - `searchParams` typed as `Promise>` - Call query functions directly (no API routes) - Pass data down to Client Components (`QuoteTable`, `QuoteForm`, etc.) - No `loading.tsx` or `error.tsx` files present --- ## 4. Email (Resend) Setup **File:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/shared/lib/email.ts` Lazy singleton pattern — `getResend()` initializes `new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY)` on first call and returns `null` if the key is missing (graceful no-op, logs a warning). Env vars used: - `RESEND_API_KEY` — required - `ADMIN_EMAIL` — defaults to `"hello@pragmatech.ai"` - `FROM_EMAIL` — defaults to `"PRAGMA "` Two existing email functions: - `sendContactNotification(data)` — sends new contact form submission to admin - `sendQuoteToClient(data: { clientEmail, clientName, quoteTitle, quoteUrl })` — sends quote-ready notification to client Email templates are inline HTML strings (no React Email or external template library). Both use a shared dark-themed HTML layout (`background: #0a0a0a`, cyan accent `#0ff`). There is a local `escapeHtml()` helper in the same file. **Pattern to reuse for a new AI quote email:** ```ts await client.emails.send({ from: FROM_EMAIL, to: recipientEmail, subject: `...`, html: `
...
`, }); ``` --- ## 5. LibSQL Client Setup **File:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/shared/lib/db.ts` Lazy singleton via module-level `let client: Client | null = null`. `getDb()` initializes on first call using: ```ts createClient({ url: process.env.DATABASE_URL || "file:local.db", authToken: process.env.DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN || undefined, }) ``` `file:local.db` is the local dev fallback — matches the `local.db` file in the project root. **Query pattern — raw SQL only, no ORM:** ```ts const result = await db.execute({ sql: "SELECT * FROM quotes WHERE id = ?", args: [id], }); // result.rows is InValue[][], cast manually: row as Record ``` All queries go through `ensureDb()` in queries.ts (calls `initDb()` then `getDb()`), which means the schema is created/migrated lazily on the first DB access in any given process. No query builder, no Drizzle, no Kysely. --- ## Key Patterns to Reuse for AI Quote System 1. **New column:** Add to `initDb()` CREATE TABLE + append an `ALTER TABLE` migration in the `migrations[]` array. 2. **New field on Quote type:** Add optional field to `Quote` interface in `types/index.ts`, add to `quoteFormSchema` in `schemas.ts`, map it in `rowToQuote()` in `queries.ts`, include it in the `INSERT`/`UPDATE` SQL in `createQuote`/`updateQuote`. 3. **New action:** Add `"use server"` function to `actions/index.ts`, parse input, call query, `revalidatePath()`. 4. **New email:** Add a function to `src/shared/lib/email.ts` following the `sendQuoteToClient` pattern — call `getResend()`, guard on null, call `client.emails.send()`. 5. **New admin page:** Async Server Component, `await getTranslations("Admin")`, fetch directly from queries, render Client Components.

content.text:
Here is the comprehensive report on all six areas:

---

## 1. PDF Generation (`@react-pdf/renderer`)

**Files involved:**
- `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/features/quotes/components/QuotePdfDocument.tsx` — the React-PDF template component
- `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/app/api/quote/[id]/pdf/route.ts` — the API route that serves the PDF

**How it works:**

The API route is a `GET` handler at `/api/quote/[id]/pdf`. It:
1. Applies IP-based rate limiting (30 req/min via `rateLimit()`)
2. Fetches the quote from LibSQL using `getQuoteById(id)`
3. Calls `renderToBuffer(QuotePdfDocument({ quote }))` (note: called as a function, not JSX)
4. Returns a `Response` with `Content-Type: application/pdf` and `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="pragma-{client-slug}-{title-slug}.pdf"`

The PDF download is triggered from the client via a plain `<a href="/api/quote/${quote.id}/pdf" download>` anchor tag inside `QuoteViewer`.

**Two PDF templates exist inside `QuotePdfDocument.tsx`:**

- `BlueprintPdfDocument` — for `quoteType === "blueprint"`: narrative layout with problem/opportunity sections, deliverables list, timeline+investment side-by-side boxes, next steps, and scope note. Header labels are hardcoded in Spanish ("PROPUESTA", "FECHA", "VÁLIDO HASTA", "Preparado para", "Entregables", etc.).
- Inline default layout — for `quoteType === "line-items"`: classic table-based quote with Description/Qty/Unit Price/Total columns, subtotal/discount/grand total rows, and a notes section. Labels are in English.

**Design tokens in the PDF** use a `colors` object:
```ts
const colors = {
  bg: "#0a0a0f", surface: "#12121a", border: "#1e1e2e",
  accent: "#00f0ff", text: "#e4e4e7", muted: "#71717a",
};
```
The accent (`#00f0ff`) is used for logo color, header bottom border, grand total top border, and deliverable numbers. The `LogoMark` component renders a cyan square with "P" + "PRAGMA" text — no actual image asset is used.

---

## 2. i18n (next-intl)

**Config files:**
- `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/i18n/routing.ts` — defines locales `["en", "es"]` with `defaultLocale: "en"` via `defineRouting()`
- `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/i18n/request.ts` — `getRequestConfig()` dynamically imports `src/messages/{locale}.json`
- `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/i18n/navigation.ts` — re-exports locale-aware `Link`, `redirect`, `usePathname`, `useRouter`, `getPathname` from `createNavigation(routing)`

**Translation files:**
- `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/messages/en.json`
- `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/messages/es.json`

Both files contain top-level keys: `Navbar`, `Hero`, `Services`, `About`, `CaseStudies`, `Testimonials`, `Contact`, `Footer`, `Quote`, `Admin`. The `Quote` namespace is fully bilingual and covers all quote page strings (status labels, action buttons, field labels, blueprint-specific labels like `theProblem`, `theOpportunity`, `deliverables`, `investment`, `nextSteps`, `scopeNote`).

**Server-side pattern** (`getTranslations`): used in async server components, e.g.:
```ts
// src/app/[locale]/admin/page.tsx
const t = await getTranslations("Admin");
```

**Client-side pattern** (`useTranslations`): used in `"use client"` components, e.g.:
```ts
// src/features/quotes/components/QuoteViewer.tsx
const t = useTranslations("Quote");
```

**Provider setup:** `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/app/[locale]/layout.tsx` wraps children in `<NextIntlClientProvider locale={locale} messages={messages}>` after validating the locale with `hasLocale()`.

**Locale switcher:** `QuoteHeader` inside `QuoteViewer` renders two `<Link>` elements with explicit `locale="en"` / `locale="es"` props, pointing to the same `/quote/${quoteId}` path, switching the active locale.

---

## 3. Client-Facing Quote Page

**Route:** `/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx`
Path: `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/app/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx`

```ts
export default async function QuotePage({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ id: string }>;
}) {
  const { id } = await params;
  const quote = await getQuoteById(id);
  if (!quote) notFound();
  return <QuoteViewer quote={quote} />;
}
```

This is a **pure server component** — it awaits `params`, calls `getQuoteById(id)` directly (no fetch, no cache layer), and passes the full `Quote` object as a prop to the client component `QuoteViewer`. It sets `robots: { index: false, follow: false }` to prevent search engine indexing.

**Data loaded:** The full `Quote` record from the `quotes` LibSQL table, deserialized by `rowToQuote()` which parses JSON columns (`line_items`, `deliverables`, `next_steps`) and maps snake_case DB columns to camelCase TypeScript fields.

---

## 4. Component Patterns

**Server component data-fetching pattern** (seen in admin page and quote page):
- Async page functions directly `await` DB query functions (no API intermediary)
- Props are awaited: `const { id } = await params`; `const params = await searchParams`
- DB data is passed directly as props to client components

**Client component pattern** (seen in `QuoteViewer`, `QuoteForm`, etc.):
- Marked with `"use client"` at the top
- Receive the full typed `Quote` object (or slices) as props
- Use `useTranslations()` and `useLocale()` from next-intl
- Use locale-aware `useRouter()` from `@/i18n/navigation`
- Server Actions (`"use server"` functions in `src/features/quotes/actions/index.ts`) are called directly — e.g., `await acceptQuoteAction(quote.id)` — followed by `router.refresh()` to re-render from the server

**Feature folder structure:**
```
src/features/quotes/
  actions/index.ts       — server actions (createQuote, updateQuote, deleteQuote, acceptQuote, sendQuote)
  components/
    QuoteViewer.tsx      — client component, renders quote to user
    QuoteForm.tsx        — client component, admin form
    QuotePdfDocument.tsx — React-PDF template
    QuoteTable.tsx       — client component
    QuoteFilters.tsx     — client component
  lib/
    queries.ts           — DB query functions (getQuoteById, getAllQuotes, etc.)
    schemas.ts           — Zod validation schemas
  types/index.ts         — Quote interface, utility functions
```

---

## 5. Tailwind 4 Setup

**No `tailwind.config.ts` exists.** Tailwind 4 is configured entirely via CSS using the `@theme inline` block.

File: `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/app/globals.css`

```css
@import "tailwindcss";

@theme inline {
  --color-pragma-bg: #0a0a0f;
  --color-pragma-surface: #12121a;
  --color-pragma-border: #1e1e2e;
  --color-pragma-accent: #00f0ff;      /* primary brand color — cyan, NOT #00FFD1 */
  --color-pragma-accent-2: #7c3aed;   /* purple */
  --color-pragma-accent-3: #00ff88;   /* green */
  --color-pragma-text: #e4e4e7;
  --color-pragma-muted: #71717a;

  --font-display: "Space Grotesk", system-ui, sans-serif;
  --font-sans: "Inter", system-ui, sans-serif;
  --font-mono: "JetBrains Mono", monospace;
}
```

These tokens map directly to Tailwind utility classes: `bg-pragma-bg`, `text-pragma-accent`, `border-pragma-border`, `font-display`, etc. — used extensively throughout all components.

**Pragma brand accent is `#00f0ff` (cyan), not `#00FFD1`.** There are three accent colors total: cyan (`#00f0ff`), purple (`#7c3aed`), and green (`#00ff88`).

**Fonts** are loaded in the root layout via `next/font/google` (Space Grotesk, Inter, JetBrains Mono) with CSS variable injection.

**Custom utility classes** defined at the bottom of globals.css: `.bg-grid` (dot grid), `.bg-gradient-mesh` (radial gradient overlays), `.glow-cyan/purple/green` (box-shadow glows), and custom scrollbar styles.

---

## 6. File Structure Under `/app`

```
src/app/
├── favicon.ico
├── globals.css
├── layout.tsx                        ← root layout: fonts, Vercel Analytics/SpeedInsights
├── page.tsx                          ← root page (likely redirects to [locale])
├── robots.ts
├── sitemap.ts
├── [locale]/                         ← locale-scoped routing segment
│   ├── layout.tsx                    ← NextIntlClientProvider + ToastProvider
│   ├── page.tsx                      ← landing page (marketing)
│   ├── admin/
│   │   ├── layout.tsx                ← admin auth gate layout
│   │   ├── page.tsx                  ← admin dashboard (quotes list + filters)
│   │   ├── contacts/
│   │   │   └── page.tsx              ← contact submissions viewer
│   │   └── quotes/
│   │       ├── new/page.tsx          ← new quote form
│   │       └── [id]/edit/page.tsx    ← edit quote form
│   └── quote/
│       └── [id]/
│           └── page.tsx              ← public client-facing quote viewer
└── api/
    ├── dev/                          ← (dev-only routes)
    └── quote/
        └── [id]/
            └── pdf/
                └── route.ts          ← GET /api/quote/[id]/pdf — PDF download
```

---

## Key Implications for the AI-Generated Custom Quote System

1. **AI content fields already exist in the DB schema** — `problem`, `opportunity`, `deliverables` (JSON), `timeline`, `fixedPrice`, `listPrice`, `preferentialNote`, `paymentTerms`, `nextSteps`, `scopeNote` — all the fields an AI generator would populate for a "blueprint" type quote.

2. **The PDF template for blueprint quotes is already built** and renders all those fields. An AI generation step would just need to produce a `QuoteFormData`-shaped object matching the `quoteFormSchema` in `/Users/santiagocoronado/PRAGMA/pragma-web/src/features/quotes/lib/schemas.ts`.

3. **The brand accent is `#00f0ff`** (cyan), not `#00FFD1`. The PDF uses this for all accent elements. You'd keep this consistent in any new template.

4. **The blueprint PDF labels are hardcoded in Spanish** while the web viewer uses next-intl translations. For an AI-generated quote you may want to unify these by accepting the `quote.locale` in the PDF renderer too.

5. **Server actions pattern:** new AI generation logic should be a `"use server"` action in the `src/features/quotes/actions/` folder, calling `createQuote()` with AI-generated data and returning the new `id` for redirect.

I need a detailed implementation plan for replacing a rigid quote system with an AI-generated custom quote pipeline. Here's everything I know about the codebase and the spec.

## Project Stack
- Next.js 16 App Router, React 19, TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS 4 (config via CSS `@theme inline`, NOT tailwind.config.ts)
- LibSQL (Turso) via `@libsql/client` — raw SQL only, no ORM
- next-intl for i18n (en/es), Zod for validation
- Resend for email, @react-pdf/renderer for PDF
- Vitest + Playwright for testing

## Existing Architecture (Key Files)

### DB Layer
- `src/shared/lib/db.ts` — lazy singleton DB client, schema bootstrap via `initDb()`, inline ALTER TABLE migrations array. No separate migration files.
- `src/features/quotes/lib/queries.ts` — `getAllQuotes()`, `getQuoteById()`, `createQuote()`, `updateQuote()`, `deleteQuote()`, `updateQuoteStatus()`. Raw SQL pattern: `db.execute({ sql, args: [] })`. `rowToQuote()` helper centralizes row mapping.
- `src/features/quotes/types/index.ts` — `Quote` interface, camelCase fields
- `src/features/quotes/lib/schemas.ts` — Zod `quoteFormSchema`

### Admin Area  
- Auth gate in `src/app/[locale]/admin/layout.tsx`
- Pattern: async Server Component, `await getTranslations("Admin")`, direct DB queries, pass data to Client Components
- Current routes: `/admin` (dashboard+list), `/admin/quotes/new`, `/admin/quotes/[id]/edit`

### Client-Facing Quote Page
- `src/app/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx` — awaits `params`, calls `getQuoteById(id)`, passes `Quote` to `QuoteViewer` Client Component

### PDF Generation
- `src/features/quotes/components/QuotePdfDocument.tsx` — renders blueprint/line-items quotes
- `src/app/api/quote/[id]/pdf/route.ts` — rate-limited GET handler, calls `renderToBuffer(QuotePdfDocument({ quote }))`
- Quote download triggered from `<a href="/api/quote/${id}/pdf" download>` in QuoteViewer

### Email
- `src/shared/lib/email.ts` — lazy Resend singleton via `getResend()`, returns null if no API key
- `sendQuoteToClient({ clientEmail, clientName, quoteTitle, quoteUrl })` — already exists
- Inline HTML email templates (no React Email)

### Current DB Schema (quotes table)
The existing table has type-specific columns: `quote_type`, `line_items`, `problem`, `opportunity`, `deliverables`, `timeline`, `fixed_price`, `list_price`, `preferential_note`, `payment_terms`, `next_steps`, `scope_note`, plus core fields: `id`, `client_name`, `client_email`, `client_company`, `title`, `status`, `locale`, `currency`, `valid_until`, `created_at`, `updated_at`.

### Tailwind 4 Design Tokens (in globals.css)
```css
--color-pragma-bg: #0a0a0f;
--color-pragma-surface: #12121a;
--color-pragma-border: #1e1e2e;
--color-pragma-accent: #00f0ff;
--color-pragma-accent-2: #7c3aed;
--color-pragma-accent-3: #00ff88;
--color-pragma-text: #e4e4e7;
--color-pragma-muted: #71717a;
--font-display: "Space Grotesk", system-ui, sans-serif;
```

## What Needs to Be Built (from spec)

### New DB columns needed (add to existing table, keep old columns for backward compat):
```sql
raw_content TEXT    -- the original markdown I wrote
generated_component TEXT  -- the generated component source code (for reference)
```
Plus: `locale` and `currency` already exist. No DROP of existing columns yet.

### New admin routes:
- `/admin/quotes/page.tsx` — dedicated list (separate from admin dashboard)
- `/admin/quotes/[id]/page.tsx` — admin preview of a specific quote (not edit, just view with action bar: Send, Copy link, Delete, Back)

### Modify client-facing route:
- `src/app/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx` — if quote has AI-generated component, render it; else render legacy QuoteViewer

### PDF export:
- New PDF template for AI quotes that parses raw_content
- Existing route `api/quote/[id]/pdf/route.ts` needs to branch on quote type

### Email:
- Reuse `sendQuoteToClient()` from email.ts
- New server action `sendAiQuoteAction(id)` or update existing sendQuoteAction

### Generation Pipeline (the core innovation):
- A Claude Code skill at `.claude/commands/generate-quote.md`
- When run with a `.md` file, Claude Code:
  1. Parses frontmatter (client_name, client_email, client_company, title, currency, locale, valid_until)
  2. Generates a bespoke React component: `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/QuoteContent.tsx` using Tailwind 4 classes
  3. Generates a PDF template: `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/QuotePDF.tsx` using @react-pdf/renderer
  4. Updates a registry file `src/generated-quotes/registry.ts` (statically importable, webpack-friendly)
  5. Runs a script `scripts/create-quote.ts` to insert the DB row

## Key Design Decisions I Need You to Evaluate

1. **Component storage format**: Should `generated_component` store:
   a. The React component source code (.tsx) as a string (reference only, actual serving via file on disk)
   b. Pre-rendered HTML string (served via dangerouslySetInnerHTML, avoids file loading complexity)
   c. Something else?

2. **Runtime loading of generated components**: The cleanest approach I've identified:
   - Generated files live in `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/QuoteContent.tsx`
   - A `registry.ts` file exports all of them statically: `export const quoteRegistry = { 'abc123': { QuoteContent, QuotePDF }, ... }`
   - `page.tsx` imports from registry and looks up by id
   - Generation pipeline updates registry.ts when creating a new quote
   - Requires a redeploy to production after adding a new quote (acceptable — developer workflow)
   
   Is this the right approach? What are the tradeoffs?

3. **PDF for AI quotes**: Two options:
   a. Each quote has its own `QuotePDF.tsx` in `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/` (loaded via registry, same pattern as web component)
   b. A single generic `AiQuotePdfDocument.tsx` that parses raw_content and renders it
   
   Which is better?

4. **DB insertion script**: Should this be a standalone `scripts/create-quote.ts` that's called by the generation pipeline, or should it be integrated differently?

Please provide:
- Concrete file paths for every file to create/modify
- The exact changes needed (function signatures, component props, SQL additions)
- The generation pipeline workflow step by step
- Any risks or gotchas I should watch for

import { createClient, type Client } from "@libsql/client";

let client: Client | null = null;

export function getDb(): Client {
  if (!client) {
    const url = process.env.DATABASE_URL || "file:local.db";
    const authToken = process.env.DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN || undefined;

    client = createClient({ url, authToken });
  }
  return client;
}

export async function initDb() {
  const db = getDb();

  await db.execute(`
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS quotes (
      id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
      client_name TEXT NOT NULL,
      client_email TEXT NOT NULL,
      client_company TEXT DEFAULT '',
      title TEXT NOT NULL,
      description TEXT DEFAULT '',
      quote_type TEXT DEFAULT 'line-items',
      line_items TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]',
      currency TEXT DEFAULT 'MXN',
      discount REAL DEFAULT 0,
      notes TEXT DEFAULT '',
      status TEXT DEFAULT 'draft',
      valid_until TEXT DEFAULT '',
      locale TEXT DEFAULT 'en',
      problem TEXT,
      opportunity TEXT,
      deliverables TEXT,
      timeline TEXT,
      fixed_price REAL,
      list_price REAL,
      preferential_note TEXT,
      payment_terms TEXT,
      next_steps TEXT,
      scope_note TEXT,
      created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
      updated_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
    )
  `);

  // Migrations for existing installations (ignore errors if columns already exist)
  const migrations = [
    "ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN quote_type TEXT DEFAULT 'line-items'",
    "ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN problem TEXT",
    "ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN opportunity TEXT",
    "ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN deliverables TEXT",
    "ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN timeline TEXT",

tool_input.content:
# Plan: AI-Generated Custom Quote Pipeline

## Context

Pragma's current quote system has two hardcoded types ("line-items" and "blueprint") with type-specific DB columns, Zod schemas, and UI components. Every new project type requires a schema change and new components — unsustainable. The solution: replace this with a pipeline where a `.md` file is fed to Claude Code, which generates a **bespoke React component per quote** and inserts a DB row. Clients receive a link to a polished, hand-crafted page. No LLM at runtime — component is pre-generated and committed to the codebase.

---

## Architecture Decision: Static Registry Pattern

Generated components live in `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/`. A **`registry.ts`** file exports all of them statically — this is webpack-compatible (no dynamic `import()` with computed paths, which breaks bundlers). The generation pipeline updates the registry file when it creates a new quote. A Vercel redeploy is required for new quotes to appear in production — acceptable for this developer-only workflow.

`generated_component` DB column stores the component source code as a reference snapshot.

---

## Files to Create

```
src/generated-quotes/registry.ts                        ← Static registry (starts empty, pipeline appends)
src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/page.tsx                  ← Dedicated quotes list
src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/[id]/page.tsx             ← Admin preview + action bar
src/features/quotes/components/AiQuoteShell.tsx         ← Client wrapper (header, locale switch, accept, footer)
src/features/quotes/components/AdminQuoteActionBar.tsx  ← Send / Copy link / Delete / Back actions
scripts/create-quote.ts                                 ← DB insertion script (called by generation pipeline)
.claude/commands/generate-quote.md                      ← Claude Code skill
```

## Files to Modify

```
src/shared/lib/db.ts                      ← Append 2 migrations
src/features/quotes/types/index.ts        ← Add "ai-generated" to QuoteType union; add rawContent, generatedComponent fields
src/features/quotes/lib/queries.ts        ← rowToQuote() + new createAiQuote() function
src/features/quotes/actions/index.ts      ← Add sendAiQuoteAction(id)
src/features/quotes/components/QuoteTable.tsx ← Add "AI Custom" badge for "ai-generated" type
src/app/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx      ← Branch: AI quotes → AiQuoteShell; legacy → QuoteViewer
src/app/api/quote/[id]/pdf/route.ts       ← Branch: AI quotes use registry QuotePDF
src/messages/en.json + es.json            ← Add "aiGeneratedType", admin action strings
```

---

## Phase 1: DB + Types (Foundation)

### `src/shared/lib/db.ts`
Append to migrations array (try/catch already handles "column exists"):
```ts
"ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN raw_content TEXT",
"ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN generated_component TEXT",
```

### `src/features/quotes/types/index.ts`
```ts
export type QuoteType = "line-items" | "blueprint" | "ai-generated";

// Add to Quote interface:
rawContent?: string;
generatedComponent?: string;
```

### `src/features/quotes/lib/queries.ts`
In `rowToQuote()`, append:
```ts
rawContent: (row.raw_content as string) || undefined,
generatedComponent: (row.generated_component as string) || undefined,
```

Add `createAiQuote(data: AiQuoteInput): Promise<Quote>` — uses `nanoid(10)` for id, inserts with `quote_type = 'ai-generated'`, `line_items = '[]'`, and the two new columns. Does NOT touch `quoteFormSchema` (avoids breaking the admin form).

```ts
export interface AiQuoteInput {
  clientName: string; clientEmail: string; clientCompany: string;
  title: string; currency: Quote["currency"]; locale: Quote["locale"];
  validUntil: string; rawContent: string; generatedComponent: string;
}
```

---

## Phase 2: Static Registry Infrastructure

### `src/generated-quotes/registry.ts`
```ts
// Auto-generated by .claude/commands/generate-quote.md — do not edit manually.
import type { ComponentType } from "react";
import type { Quote } from "@/features/quotes/types";

export interface QuoteRegistryEntry {
  QuoteContent: ComponentType<{ quote: Quote }>;
  QuotePDF: ComponentType<{ quote: Quote }>;
}

export const quoteRegistry: Record<string, QuoteRegistryEntry> = {
  // Pipeline appends entries here: 'abc123xyz': { QuoteContent, QuotePDF }
};
```

When the pipeline runs, it prepends two named imports and adds an entry key:
```ts
import { QuoteContent as QuoteContent_abc123 } from "./Quote_abc123/QuoteContent";
import { QuotePDF as QuotePDF_abc123 } from "./Quote_abc123/QuotePDF";
// adds to quoteRegistry: 'abc123xyz': { QuoteContent: QuoteContent_abc123, QuotePDF: QuotePDF_abc123 }
```

---

## Phase 3: Client-Facing Route

### `src/app/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx`
```ts
import { quoteRegistry } from "@/generated-quotes/registry";
import { AiQuoteShell } from "@/features/quotes/components/AiQuoteShell";

// After fetching quote:
if (quote.quoteType === "ai-generated") {
  const entry = quoteRegistry[quote.id];
  if (!entry) notFound();
  return <AiQuoteShell quote={quote} QuoteContent={entry.QuoteContent} />;
}
return <QuoteViewer quote={quote} />;
```

### `src/features/quotes/components/AiQuoteShell.tsx`
Client Component (`"use client"`). Props: `{ quote: Quote, QuoteContent: ComponentType<{ quote: Quote }> }`.

Renders:
1. **PRAGMA header** with locale switcher (Link with `locale="en"/"es"` props — same pattern as existing QuoteViewer's `QuoteHeader`)
2. `<QuoteContent quote={quote} />` — the bespoke AI-generated component
3. **Action bar**: Accept button (calls `acceptQuoteAction`, then `router.refresh()`) + PDF download link (`<a href="/api/quote/${quote.id}/pdf" download>`)
4. **Footer**: "Powered by PRAGMA" + valid_until date

Extract the `QuoteActions` pattern from `QuoteViewer.tsx` to avoid duplication.

---

## Phase 4: PDF Export

### `src/app/api/quote/[id]/pdf/route.ts`
After fetching quote, branch:
```ts
if (quote.quoteType === "ai-generated") {
  const { quoteRegistry } = await import("@/generated-quotes/registry");
  const entry = quoteRegistry[quote.id];
  if (!entry) return Response.json({ error: "not found" }, { status: 404 });
  const buffer = await renderToBuffer(entry.QuotePDF({ quote }));
  // ... same filename/Content-Type headers as existing
}
```
Use dynamic `import()` here (not top-level) — avoids eager bundling of all PDF templates for routes that serve legacy quotes.

Each AI quote's `QuotePDF.tsx` is a `@react-pdf/renderer` component generated by the pipeline alongside `QuoteContent.tsx`.

---

## Phase 5: Admin Pages

### `src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/page.tsx`
Mirrors the quote-list section of `src/app/[locale]/admin/page.tsx`. Async Server Component pattern: `await getTranslations("Admin")`, `await getAllQuotes(filters)`, render `<QuoteTable>`, `<QuoteFilters>`, `<Pagination>`. No "Create Quote" button.

### `src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/[id]/page.tsx`
Async Server Component. Fetches quote, checks it's `"ai-generated"` (else 404). Renders:
1. Top bar with `<AdminQuoteActionBar quote={quote} />` (Client Component)
2. `<entry.QuoteContent quote={quote} />` (preview — same render as client sees)

Falls back to a "Registry not updated — redeploy to activate" message if `quoteRegistry[quote.id]` is undefined.

### `src/features/quotes/components/AdminQuoteActionBar.tsx`
Client Component. Actions:
- **Send**: calls `sendAiQuoteAction(quote.id)` → `router.refresh()`
- **Copy link**: `navigator.clipboard.writeText(quoteUrl)`
- **Delete**: calls `deleteQuoteAction(quote.id)` → redirect to `/admin/quotes`
- **Back**: `<Link href="/admin/quotes">`

### `src/features/quotes/actions/index.ts`
Add:
```ts
export async function sendAiQuoteAction(id: string) {
  const quote = await getQuoteById(id);
  if (!quote) return { error: "not found" };
  const locale = quote.locale ?? "en";
  const baseUrl = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000";
  const quoteUrl = `${baseUrl}/${locale}/quote/${quote.id}`;
  await sendQuoteToClient({ clientEmail: quote.clientEmail, clientName: quote.clientName, quoteTitle: quote.title, quoteUrl });
  await updateQuoteStatus(id, "sent");
  revalidatePath("/[locale]/admin/quotes", "page");
  revalidatePath(`/[locale]/admin/quotes/${id}`, "page");
}
```

### `src/features/quotes/components/QuoteTable.tsx`
Add `"ai-generated"` branch in the type badge display:
```ts
quoteType === "ai-generated" ? t("aiGeneratedType") : quoteType === "blueprint" ? ...
```

---

## Phase 6: Generation Pipeline Skill

### `scripts/create-quote.ts`
Standalone Node.js script (run via `npx tsx scripts/create-quote.ts`).

CLI args: `--id`, `--file` (path to `.md`), `--component` (TSX source code string, optional).

Uses `gray-matter` (add as devDependency) to parse frontmatter. Creates `@libsql/client` directly (same env vars as `src/shared/lib/db.ts`). Calls `createAiQuote()` equivalent SQL inline (can't import from `src/` in a Node script without tsconfig path resolution). Prints the quote ID and client-facing URL on success.

Add to `package.json` scripts:
```json
"create-quote": "dotenv -e .env.local -- tsx scripts/create-quote.ts"
```

### `.claude/commands/generate-quote.md`
Claude Code skill invoked as `/generate-quote path/to/spec.md`.

Steps the skill instructs Claude to perform:
1. Read the `.md` file → parse frontmatter (client_name, client_email, client_company, title, currency, locale, valid_until) + body
2. Generate a **nanoid-style 10-char ID** (e.g., `xK9mP2qR4s`)
3. Create directory `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/`
4. Generate **`QuoteContent.tsx`**: Named export `QuoteContent`. Client Component (`"use client"`). Props `{ quote: Quote }`. Uses only Tailwind 4 classes with Pragma tokens (`bg-pragma-bg`, `text-pragma-accent`, `font-display`, etc.) — **no tailwind.config.ts**. Layout is bespoke to the content: pricing tables, deliverable cards, visual timelines — not a generic renderer. Does NOT include header, nav, locale switcher, or action buttons (those come from `AiQuoteShell`).
5. Generate **`QuotePDF.tsx`**: Named export `QuotePDF`. Uses `@react-pdf/renderer` (`Document`, `Page`, `View`, `Text`, `StyleSheet`). Props `{ quote: Quote }`. Uses hex colors directly (no CSS vars in PDF). Mirrors the web layout structure as PDF primitives.
6. Update `src/generated-quotes/registry.ts`: prepend two import lines, add entry to `quoteRegistry` object. Never remove existing entries.
7. Run DB insertion: `npm run create-quote -- --id [id] --file [path]`
8. Output summary: quote ID, client URL, admin URL, files created, reminder to `git add src/generated-quotes/ && git commit && git push` for Vercel deployment.

**Design guidelines embedded in the skill**: Pragma dark theme, Space Grotesk headings, cyan `#00f0ff` accent, generous whitespace, bespoke layout decisions based on content type.

---

## Phase 7: i18n

### `src/messages/en.json` (Admin section)
```json
"aiGeneratedType": "AI Custom",
"quotePreview": "Preview",
"sendQuote": "Send to Client",
"copyLink": "Copy Link",
"backToQuotes": "← Quotes"
```
Mirror keys in `es.json`.

---

## Verification Plan

1. **DB migration**: Start dev server, check `local.db` has `raw_content` and `generated_component` columns. Existing quotes still load at `/quote/[id]` via the legacy `QuoteViewer` path.

tool_response.content:
# Plan: AI-Generated Custom Quote Pipeline

## Context

Pragma's current quote system has two hardcoded types ("line-items" and "blueprint") with type-specific DB columns, Zod schemas, and UI components. Every new project type requires a schema change and new components — unsustainable. The solution: replace this with a pipeline where a `.md` file is fed to Claude Code, which generates a **bespoke React component per quote** and inserts a DB row. Clients receive a link to a polished, hand-crafted page. No LLM at runtime — component is pre-generated and committed to the codebase.

---

## Architecture Decision: Static Registry Pattern

Generated components live in `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/`. A **`registry.ts`** file exports all of them statically — this is webpack-compatible (no dynamic `import()` with computed paths, which breaks bundlers). The generation pipeline updates the registry file when it creates a new quote. A Vercel redeploy is required for new quotes to appear in production — acceptable for this developer-only workflow.

`generated_component` DB column stores the component source code as a reference snapshot.

---

## Files to Create

```
src/generated-quotes/registry.ts                        ← Static registry (starts empty, pipeline appends)
src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/page.tsx                  ← Dedicated quotes list
src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/[id]/page.tsx             ← Admin preview + action bar
src/features/quotes/components/AiQuoteShell.tsx         ← Client wrapper (header, locale switch, accept, footer)
src/features/quotes/components/AdminQuoteActionBar.tsx  ← Send / Copy link / Delete / Back actions
scripts/create-quote.ts                                 ← DB insertion script (called by generation pipeline)
.claude/commands/generate-quote.md                      ← Claude Code skill
```

## Files to Modify

```
src/shared/lib/db.ts                      ← Append 2 migrations
src/features/quotes/types/index.ts        ← Add "ai-generated" to QuoteType union; add rawContent, generatedComponent fields
src/features/quotes/lib/queries.ts        ← rowToQuote() + new createAiQuote() function
src/features/quotes/actions/index.ts      ← Add sendAiQuoteAction(id)
src/features/quotes/components/QuoteTable.tsx ← Add "AI Custom" badge for "ai-generated" type
src/app/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx      ← Branch: AI quotes → AiQuoteShell; legacy → QuoteViewer
src/app/api/quote/[id]/pdf/route.ts       ← Branch: AI quotes use registry QuotePDF
src/messages/en.json + es.json            ← Add "aiGeneratedType", admin action strings
```

---

## Phase 1: DB + Types (Foundation)

### `src/shared/lib/db.ts`
Append to migrations array (try/catch already handles "column exists"):
```ts
"ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN raw_content TEXT",
"ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN generated_component TEXT",
```

### `src/features/quotes/types/index.ts`
```ts
export type QuoteType = "line-items" | "blueprint" | "ai-generated";

// Add to Quote interface:
rawContent?: string;
generatedComponent?: string;
```

### `src/features/quotes/lib/queries.ts`
In `rowToQuote()`, append:
```ts
rawContent: (row.raw_content as string) || undefined,
generatedComponent: (row.generated_component as string) || undefined,
```

Add `createAiQuote(data: AiQuoteInput): Promise<Quote>` — uses `nanoid(10)` for id, inserts with `quote_type = 'ai-generated'`, `line_items = '[]'`, and the two new columns. Does NOT touch `quoteFormSchema` (avoids breaking the admin form).

```ts
export interface AiQuoteInput {
  clientName: string; clientEmail: string; clientCompany: string;
  title: string; currency: Quote["currency"]; locale: Quote["locale"];
  validUntil: string; rawContent: string; generatedComponent: string;
}
```

---

## Phase 2: Static Registry Infrastructure

### `src/generated-quotes/registry.ts`
```ts
// Auto-generated by .claude/commands/generate-quote.md — do not edit manually.
import type { ComponentType } from "react";
import type { Quote } from "@/features/quotes/types";

export interface QuoteRegistryEntry {
  QuoteContent: ComponentType<{ quote: Quote }>;
  QuotePDF: ComponentType<{ quote: Quote }>;
}

export const quoteRegistry: Record<string, QuoteRegistryEntry> = {
  // Pipeline appends entries here: 'abc123xyz': { QuoteContent, QuotePDF }
};
```

When the pipeline runs, it prepends two named imports and adds an entry key:
```ts
import { QuoteContent as QuoteContent_abc123 } from "./Quote_abc123/QuoteContent";
import { QuotePDF as QuotePDF_abc123 } from "./Quote_abc123/QuotePDF";
// adds to quoteRegistry: 'abc123xyz': { QuoteContent: QuoteContent_abc123, QuotePDF: QuotePDF_abc123 }
```

---

## Phase 3: Client-Facing Route

### `src/app/[locale]/quote/[id]/page.tsx`
```ts
import { quoteRegistry } from "@/generated-quotes/registry";
import { AiQuoteShell } from "@/features/quotes/components/AiQuoteShell";

// After fetching quote:
if (quote.quoteType === "ai-generated") {
  const entry = quoteRegistry[quote.id];
  if (!entry) notFound();
  return <AiQuoteShell quote={quote} QuoteContent={entry.QuoteContent} />;
}
return <QuoteViewer quote={quote} />;
```

### `src/features/quotes/components/AiQuoteShell.tsx`
Client Component (`"use client"`). Props: `{ quote: Quote, QuoteContent: ComponentType<{ quote: Quote }> }`.

Renders:
1. **PRAGMA header** with locale switcher (Link with `locale="en"/"es"` props — same pattern as existing QuoteViewer's `QuoteHeader`)
2. `<QuoteContent quote={quote} />` — the bespoke AI-generated component
3. **Action bar**: Accept button (calls `acceptQuoteAction`, then `router.refresh()`) + PDF download link (`<a href="/api/quote/${quote.id}/pdf" download>`)
4. **Footer**: "Powered by PRAGMA" + valid_until date

Extract the `QuoteActions` pattern from `QuoteViewer.tsx` to avoid duplication.

---

## Phase 4: PDF Export

### `src/app/api/quote/[id]/pdf/route.ts`
After fetching quote, branch:
```ts
if (quote.quoteType === "ai-generated") {
  const { quoteRegistry } = await import("@/generated-quotes/registry");
  const entry = quoteRegistry[quote.id];
  if (!entry) return Response.json({ error: "not found" }, { status: 404 });
  const buffer = await renderToBuffer(entry.QuotePDF({ quote }));
  // ... same filename/Content-Type headers as existing
}
```
Use dynamic `import()` here (not top-level) — avoids eager bundling of all PDF templates for routes that serve legacy quotes.

Each AI quote's `QuotePDF.tsx` is a `@react-pdf/renderer` component generated by the pipeline alongside `QuoteContent.tsx`.

---

## Phase 5: Admin Pages

### `src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/page.tsx`
Mirrors the quote-list section of `src/app/[locale]/admin/page.tsx`. Async Server Component pattern: `await getTranslations("Admin")`, `await getAllQuotes(filters)`, render `<QuoteTable>`, `<QuoteFilters>`, `<Pagination>`. No "Create Quote" button.

### `src/app/[locale]/admin/quotes/[id]/page.tsx`
Async Server Component. Fetches quote, checks it's `"ai-generated"` (else 404). Renders:
1. Top bar with `<AdminQuoteActionBar quote={quote} />` (Client Component)
2. `<entry.QuoteContent quote={quote} />` (preview — same render as client sees)

Falls back to a "Registry not updated — redeploy to activate" message if `quoteRegistry[quote.id]` is undefined.

### `src/features/quotes/components/AdminQuoteActionBar.tsx`
Client Component. Actions:
- **Send**: calls `sendAiQuoteAction(quote.id)` → `router.refresh()`
- **Copy link**: `navigator.clipboard.writeText(quoteUrl)`
- **Delete**: calls `deleteQuoteAction(quote.id)` → redirect to `/admin/quotes`
- **Back**: `<Link href="/admin/quotes">`

### `src/features/quotes/actions/index.ts`
Add:
```ts
export async function sendAiQuoteAction(id: string) {
  const quote = await getQuoteById(id);
  if (!quote) return { error: "not found" };
  const locale = quote.locale ?? "en";
  const baseUrl = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000";
  const quoteUrl = `${baseUrl}/${locale}/quote/${quote.id}`;
  await sendQuoteToClient({ clientEmail: quote.clientEmail, clientName: quote.clientName, quoteTitle: quote.title, quoteUrl });
  await updateQuoteStatus(id, "sent");
  revalidatePath("/[locale]/admin/quotes", "page");
  revalidatePath(`/[locale]/admin/quotes/${id}`, "page");
}
```

### `src/features/quotes/components/QuoteTable.tsx`
Add `"ai-generated"` branch in the type badge display:
```ts
quoteType === "ai-generated" ? t("aiGeneratedType") : quoteType === "blueprint" ? ...
```

---

## Phase 6: Generation Pipeline Skill

### `scripts/create-quote.ts`
Standalone Node.js script (run via `npx tsx scripts/create-quote.ts`).

CLI args: `--id`, `--file` (path to `.md`), `--component` (TSX source code string, optional).

Uses `gray-matter` (add as devDependency) to parse frontmatter. Creates `@libsql/client` directly (same env vars as `src/shared/lib/db.ts`). Calls `createAiQuote()` equivalent SQL inline (can't import from `src/` in a Node script without tsconfig path resolution). Prints the quote ID and client-facing URL on success.

Add to `package.json` scripts:
```json
"create-quote": "dotenv -e .env.local -- tsx scripts/create-quote.ts"
```

### `.claude/commands/generate-quote.md`
Claude Code skill invoked as `/generate-quote path/to/spec.md`.

Steps the skill instructs Claude to perform:
1. Read the `.md` file → parse frontmatter (client_name, client_email, client_company, title, currency, locale, valid_until) + body
2. Generate a **nanoid-style 10-char ID** (e.g., `xK9mP2qR4s`)
3. Create directory `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/`
4. Generate **`QuoteContent.tsx`**: Named export `QuoteContent`. Client Component (`"use client"`). Props `{ quote: Quote }`. Uses only Tailwind 4 classes with Pragma tokens (`bg-pragma-bg`, `text-pragma-accent`, `font-display`, etc.) — **no tailwind.config.ts**. Layout is bespoke to the content: pricing tables, deliverable cards, visual timelines — not a generic renderer. Does NOT include header, nav, locale switcher, or action buttons (those come from `AiQuoteShell`).
5. Generate **`QuotePDF.tsx`**: Named export `QuotePDF`. Uses `@react-pdf/renderer` (`Document`, `Page`, `View`, `Text`, `StyleSheet`). Props `{ quote: Quote }`. Uses hex colors directly (no CSS vars in PDF). Mirrors the web layout structure as PDF primitives.
6. Update `src/generated-quotes/registry.ts`: prepend two import lines, add entry to `quoteRegistry` object. Never remove existing entries.
7. Run DB insertion: `npm run create-quote -- --id [id] --file [path]`
8. Output summary: quote ID, client URL, admin URL, files created, reminder to `git add src/generated-quotes/ && git commit && git push` for Vercel deployment.

**Design guidelines embedded in the skill**: Pragma dark theme, Space Grotesk headings, cyan `#00f0ff` accent, generous whitespace, bespoke layout decisions based on content type.

---

## Phase 7: i18n

### `src/messages/en.json` (Admin section)
```json
"aiGeneratedType": "AI Custom",
"quotePreview": "Preview",
"sendQuote": "Send to Client",
"copyLink": "Copy Link",
"backToQuotes": "← Quotes"
```
Mirror keys in `es.json`.

---

## Verification Plan

1. **DB migration**: Start dev server, check `local.db` has `raw_content` and `generated_component` columns. Existing quotes still load at `/quote/[id]` via the legacy `QuoteViewer` path.
2. **Generation pipeline**: Run `/generate-quote` with the Texas Ribs example `.md`. Confirm: files exist in `src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/`, registry updated, DB row inserted.
3. **Client-facing route**: Open `http://localhost:3000/en/quote/[id]`. Confirm: bespoke component renders, header/footer from `AiQuoteShell` visible, Accept button calls action.
4. **PDF**: Click download or visit `/api/quote/[id]/pdf`. Confirm: PDF downloads with AI-quote-specific layout.
5. **Admin list**: Visit `/admin/quotes`. Confirm: AI quote appears with "AI Custom" badge.
6. **Admin preview**: Visit `/admin/quotes/[id]`. Confirm: preview matches client view, Send/Copy/Delete actions work.
7. **Email**: Click Send in admin. Confirm: Resend logs the email, quote status changes to "sent".
8. **Regression**: Existing blueprint/line-items quotes still render correctly at their URLs.

---

## Gotchas

- **Registry must be committed before deploy** — generation pipeline should print an explicit reminder.
- **`QuoteContent` must be `"use client"`** — it's rendered inside `AiQuoteShell` which is a Client Component.
- **`QuotePDF` only in server contexts** — never import it into a client bundle; only access via the API route.
- **`gray-matter` devDependency** needed for `scripts/create-quote.ts`.
- **`NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` env var** needed for `sendAiQuoteAction` to construct the correct quote URL — already tracked in TODO.md as pending.
- **QuoteTable badge** — add `"ai-generated"` branch to avoid showing "undefined" as the type label.
- **Admin route `[id]/page.tsx` vs `[id]/edit/page.tsx`** — no conflict in Next.js App Router; they coexist at different URL depths.

tool_input.content:
# /generate-quote

Generate a bespoke AI-customized quote from a specification file.

## Usage

```
/generate-quote path/to/spec.md
```

## What This Does

Transforms a Markdown specification file into a complete, production-ready quote:
1. Parses the spec's frontmatter and content
2. Generates a 10-character ID (nanoid style)
3. Creates two React components: `QuoteContent.tsx` (web) and `QuotePDF.tsx` (PDF export)
4. Updates `src/generated-quotes/registry.ts` to register the new components
5. Inserts a quote row into the database
6. Outputs the client-facing and admin URLs

## Spec File Format

Create a `.md` file with YAML frontmatter:

```yaml
---
client_name: "Acme Corp"
client_email: "contact@acme.com"
client_company: "Acme Corporation"
title: "AI-Powered Texas BBQ Ordering System"
currency: "USD"
locale: "en"
valid_until: "2026-05-16"
---

# Quote Content (Markdown)

Your custom content here. This becomes the bespoke quote layout.
```

### Required Frontmatter Fields
- `client_name` (string)
- `client_email` (string)
- `title` (string)
- `client_company` (string, optional)
- `currency` (USD, EUR, MXN, COP; default: MXN)
- `locale` (en, es; default: en)
- `valid_until` (ISO date; default: empty)

### Content Section
Everything after the frontmatter is parsed as markdown and becomes the basis for component design. The generated components will have bespoke layouts—not generic renderers—based on the content structure.

## Component Generation Strategy

### QuoteContent.tsx
- **Client Component** (`"use client"`)
- Named export: `export const QuoteContent = ({ quote }: { quote: Quote }) => ...`
- Tailwind 4 styling with Pragma design tokens:
  - Colors: `bg-pragma-bg`, `text-pragma-accent`, `border-pragma-border`
  - Typography: `font-display` (Space Grotesk), `font-body`
  - Spacing: Generous whitespace, Pragma-proportioned padding/margins
- Layout is **bespoke to the content**: pricing tables, deliverable cards, visual timelines, feature comparisons—custom to the quote's unique structure
- **Never** include header, nav, locale switcher, or action buttons (those come from `AiQuoteShell`)
- Render `quote.rawContent` and any other quote fields as appropriate for the design

### QuotePDF.tsx
- **Server-side only** (never imported into client bundle)
- Named export: `export const QuotePDF = ({ quote }: { quote: Quote }) => ...`
- Uses `@react-pdf/renderer` primitives:
  - `Document`, `Page`, `View`, `Text`, `StyleSheet`
- Hex colors directly (no CSS variables in PDF; use exact RGB values like `#00f0ff` for cyan)
- Mirrors the web layout structure as PDF primitives
- Font names compatible with react-pdf (e.g., `"Helvetica"`, `"Times-Roman"`)

## Implementation Steps

1. **Parse the spec file**
   - Read the `.md` file path from the command
   - Extract frontmatter (title, client name, email, company, currency, locale, valid_until)
   - Extract body content (raw markdown)

2. **Generate ID**
   - Create a 10-character ID using `nanoid(10)` pattern: e.g., `xK9mP2qR4s`

3. **Create directory structure**
   - `mkdir -p src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/`

4. **Generate QuoteContent.tsx**
   - Analyze the markdown content to determine the best layout strategy
   - Decide: Is this a pricing table? A timeline? A feature list? A custom proposal?
   - Design a bespoke React component that renders `quote` and displays the content visually
   - Use Pragma's design system: dark theme, Space Grotesk headings, cyan accent (#00f0ff)
   - Export as named `QuoteContent`

5. **Generate QuotePDF.tsx**
   - Create a parallel PDF version using `@react-pdf/renderer`
   - Match the web layout's visual hierarchy
   - Use readable font sizes and hex colors
   - Ensure printability and professionalism
   - Export as named `QuotePDF`

6. **Update registry.ts**
   - Read `src/generated-quotes/registry.ts`
   - Add two new import statements at the top:
     ```ts
     import { QuoteContent as QuoteContent_[id] } from "./Quote_[id]/QuoteContent";
     import { QuotePDF as QuotePDF_[id] } from "./Quote_[id]/QuotePDF";
     ```
   - Add entry to `quoteRegistry`:
     ```ts
     '[id]': { QuoteContent: QuoteContent_[id], QuotePDF: QuotePDF_[id] }
     ```
   - Never remove existing entries

7. **Insert database row**
   - Run: `npm run create-quote -- --id [id] --file [spec_path]`
   - Passes the spec file's frontmatter and content to the DB
   - Prints success with client and admin URLs

8. **Output summary**
   ```
   ✓ Generated [client_name] quote
   ID: [id]
   Client URL: http://localhost:3000/[locale]/quote/[id]
   Admin URL: http://localhost:3000/[locale]/admin/quotes/[id]
   
   Files created:
     - src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/QuoteContent.tsx
     - src/generated-quotes/Quote_[id]/QuotePDF.tsx
   
   Registry updated:
     - src/generated-quotes/registry.ts
   
   Database row inserted with type: ai-generated
   
   Next steps:
     git add src/generated-quotes/
     git commit -m "feat: add AI-generated quote [id]"
     git push
     vercel deploy  (or wait for auto-deploy)
   ```

## Design Guidelines

### Pragma Design System
- **Dark theme**: `#0a0e27` background, white/cyan text
- **Accent color**: Cyan `#00f0ff`
- **Typography**: Space Grotesk for headings, system font for body
- **Spacing**: 1rem, 1.5rem, 2rem, 3rem increments
- **Borders**: Subtle, low contrast (`border-opacity-20`)

### Component Patterns
- **Section cards**: Contained with subtle borders, rounded corners
- **Typography hierarchy**: h1 → h2 → h3, clear visual levels
- **Call-to-action**: Cyan background, hover effects with Framer Motion
- **Lists**: Checkmarks, icons, or simple bullets
- **Tables**: Alternating row colors, sticky headers for PDFs

### Do's and Don'ts
✓ Read the markdown content carefully—the layout should reflect the quote's unique structure  
✓ Use Pragma's brand colors and typography  
✓ Make generous use of whitespace  
✓ Ensure the PDF version is printable and professional  
✗ Don't include header/nav/footer (those come from AiQuoteShell)  
✗ Don't use dynamic imports or client-only features in the PDF component  
✗ Don't hardcode client names or dynamic data as static styles (use the `quote` prop)  

## Example: Texas Ribs BBQ Ordering System

**Spec file:**
```yaml
---
client_name: "Texas Ribs Inc"
client_email: "owner@texasribs.com"
client_company: "Texas Ribs"
title: "AI-Powered BBQ Ordering System"
currency: "USD"
locale: "en"
valid_until: "2026-05-16"
---

# Overview

We propose an AI-powered ordering system that learns customer preferences and predicts demand.

## Deliverables

1. **Menu Recommendation Engine**
   - Train on historical orders
   - Personalized suggestions per customer

2. **Demand Forecasting**
   - Predict ribs/brisket demand by day
   - Auto-trigger prep schedules

3. **Mobile App**
   - iOS/Android ordering interface
   - Real-time status updates
```

**Generated component** would show:
- Hero with title + company name
- 3 deliverable cards with icons
- Pricing section
- Timeline
- Terms & valid_until

---

## Troubleshooting

**"Registry not found"** → The quote exists in the DB but hasn't been registered. Run `git add src/generated-quotes/ && git commit && git push` then redeploy.

**"Component not rendering"** → Check that `QuoteContent` is marked `"use client"` and exports correctly.

**"PDF empty"** → Verify `QuotePDF` uses `@react-pdf/renderer` primitives, not HTML/React elements.

---

Use this skill to generate unlimited bespoke quotes without writing new DB migrations or UI components. Each quote is a hand-crafted React component, committed to the codebase and deployed to production.

# Client

# Propuesta de servicios tecnológicos

## PRAGMA → AMEXCAP

---

# 1. Resumen ejecutivo

AMEXCAP es la voz de los fondos de inversión en México, con 130 socios entre fondos de capital de riesgo, crédito privado, crecimiento, infraestructura, energía y despachos especializados. Su operación, sin embargo, todavía depende de procesos manuales que consumen tiempo del equipo y limitan la experiencia que vive el socio.

PRAGMA propone construir una **plataforma integral** que centralice la gestión de membresías, la comunicación con socios, los eventos y el sitio web, todo bajo una sola identidad de socio y un solo panel de administración. El proyecto se entrega en tres fases secuenciales para que AMEXCAP reciba funcionalidad útil desde el primer mes y pueda distribuir la inversión en el tiempo, financiando cada fase conforme entrega valor en lugar de absorber el costo total de un solo golpe.

---

# 2. Situación actual

Durante la llamada de descubrimiento se identificaron los siguientes retos operativos:

1. **Renovación de membresías manual:** cada año el equipo contacta uno por uno a los 130 socios por correo, valida manualmente el tamaño de cada firma, reasigna la cuota correspondiente, envía la documentación y genera facturas a mano.
2. **Portal de socios abandonado:** el número de membresía que se le asigna a cada socio rara vez se usa; la mayoría lo pierde u olvida, y el equipo termina enviando todo por correo de cualquier manera.
3. **Registro de eventos desconectado:** para cada evento se contratan proveedores externos que construyen sitio y tienda de boletos desde cero, sin integración con la membresía.
4. **Eventos cerrados difíciles de coordinar:** la única forma de invitar a socios es por correo, WhatsApp y llamadas, persiguiéndolos uno por uno.
5. **Baja respuesta en la recolección de datos:** los Excel trimestrales que se envían a los fondos casi no se contestan porque los fondos no confían en cómo se manejará su información.
6. **Sitio web desactualizado:** el sitio actual no refleja el nivel de la asociación ni se integra con el resto de la operación.

---

# 3. Solución propuesta: una sola plataforma

La mayoría de estos retos son síntomas de un mismo problema de fondo: **AMEXCAP cuenta con sistemas, pero ninguno es efectivo ni cubre todas las funcionalidades que la asociación necesita.** Algunos están abandonados, otros funcionan parcialmente, y ninguno habla con los demás. Cada proceso termina viviendo en un canal distinto (correo, Excel, proveedores externos, WhatsApp), sin un hilo común que los conecte.

PRAGMA propone una **plataforma integral** que funcione como el sistema nervioso central de la asociación: una sola identidad de socio que conecte renovaciones, pagos, eventos, publicaciones, recolección de datos y comunicaciones. No son seis herramientas separadas, es una sola solución que ataca la raíz compartida de todos los problemas.

---

# 4. Hoja de ruta por fases

## Fase 1 — Cimientos de la plataforma

**Problemas que resuelve:** #1 (renovación manual), #2 (portal abandonado), #4 (eventos cerrados difíciles de coordinar) y #5 (baja respuesta en recolección de datos).

Esta primera fase construye el corazón del sistema: el backend, el panel de administración y el panel de socios. Es el módulo que reemplaza la operación manual del día a día — cómo se cobran las cuotas, cómo se comunica el equipo con los socios, dónde viven los documentos y cómo los fondos comparten información con la asociación. Sin esta base, ninguna de las fases siguientes puede existir; con ella, el equipo de AMEXCAP recupera control sobre su operación y los socios estrenan una experiencia digital que hoy no tienen.

**Alcance:**

- **Backend completo y panel de administración para el equipo de AMEXCAP**, donde gestionan socios, cuotas, documentos y comunicaciones desde un solo lugar — el centro de control que el equipo nunca ha tenido, donde finalmente cada socio, cada cuota y cada documento viven en un mismo sistema en lugar de estar dispersos en correos y hojas de Excel.
- **Panel personal para cada socio**, con su estatus de membresía, su nivel de cuota, su fecha de renovación, sus documentos e historial — la primera vez que el socio tiene una razón real para entrar a la plataforma, porque encuentra todo lo suyo en un solo lugar y deja de depender de pedírselo al equipo por correo.
- **Sistema de autenticación moderno** (correo + contraseña, o acceso por enlace) que reemplaza el número de membresía actual — los socios entran como entran a cualquier servicio digital serio, sin tener que recordar un número que siempre olvidan.
- **Automatización completa del ciclo de renovación anual**: notificaciones automáticas, validación del tamaño de firma, asignación de cuota por rango, generación de documentos y facturación — en enero, el equipo deja de escribir 130 correos individuales; lo que hoy toma semanas pasa a tomar días, y libera al equipo para enfocarse en lo que sí mueve a la asociación.
- **Sistema seguro de documentos donde los socios pueden compartir estudios y datos con la asociación**, con garantías visibles de confidencialidad, cifrado y manejo agregado de la información — los fondos por fin tienen un entorno en el que confían para compartir información sensible, lo que ataca de raíz la baja tasa de respuesta de los Excel trimestrales.
- **Notificaciones automáticas multicanal** por correo electrónico, SMS y WhatsApp — boletines, recordatorios de renovación, avisos de eventos y comunicaciones generales llegan al socio por el canal que más usa, no por el que al equipo le toca enviar a mano.
- **Web app de prueba que expone la funcionalidad desde el primer mes**, para que AMEXCAP vea, toque y valide el sistema sin esperar al final del proyecto — no compran una promesa, ven avances reales desde el inicio.

**Resultado esperado:** el equipo de AMEXCAP deja de operar como una agencia de correos en enero. Los socios estrenan una plataforma con la que sí quieren interactuar. Y por primera vez, los fondos cuentan con un canal seguro para alimentar a la asociación con la información que la industria necesita.

**Duración estimada:** 2 a 3 meses

---

## Fase 2 — Reemplazo del sitio web y migración completa

**Problemas que resuelve:** #6 (sitio web desactualizado) y consolidación del ecosistema sobre la nueva plataforma.

Esta fase trae la cara pública de AMEXCAP al mismo nivel que la operación interna. Una vez que el equipo está usando el panel nuevo y los socios entran a su portal, el siguiente paso natural es que el sitio que ve el resto del mundo refleje también esa transformación. Aquí conectamos el panel de administración y el backend de la Fase 1 al sitio público, para que toda la presencia digital de AMEXCAP — interna y externa — viva en un solo ecosistema.

**Alcance:**

- **Reemplazo completo del sitio web actual de AMEXCAP**, construido sobre el mismo backend de la Fase 1 — conectamos nuestro panel de administración y nuestro backend al sitio que ve el público, para que el sitio externo y la operación interna vivan finalmente en el mismo ecosistema y hablen el mismo idioma.
- **Diseño moderno, rápido y responsivo**, alineado al nivel y posición de AMEXCAP en la industria — la primera impresión que se llevan socios, prospectos, periodistas e inversionistas internacionales finalmente refleja lo que AMEXCAP realmente es.
- **Migración del contenido relevante del sitio anterior**, preservando lo que vale la pena conservar y dejando atrás lo que no — sin perder el patrimonio digital que ya existe, pero estrenando una base limpia.
- **Login de socios desde la portada**, conectado directamente al panel de la Fase 1 — un solo punto de entrada para todo lo que el socio necesita, sin que tenga que recordar entre qué sitio es para qué.
- **Optimización para buscadores (SEO) y para dispositivos móviles** — más visibilidad orgánica para los estudios y publicaciones de AMEXCAP, y una experiencia consistente sin importar desde dónde se conecte el socio.

**Resultado esperado:** una vez validados los cimientos en la Fase 1, todo el sitio público migra al nuevo ecosistema y queda alineado con el panel de administración. AMEXCAP deja de tener "el sistema viejo y el sistema nuevo" y pasa a tener **una sola plataforma**, lista para construir lo que sigue.

**Duración estimada:** 0.5 a 1 mes

---

## Fase 3 — Sistema de eventos

**Problemas que resuelve:** #3 (registro de eventos desconectado) y la distribución de publicaciones a socios y no socios.

Con la plataforma ya unificada, esta fase cierra el círculo construyendo el sistema de eventos sobre la base que ya existe. AMEXCAP deja de depender de proveedores externos para cada evento, los socios compran boletos con la misma cuenta que ya usan para todo lo demás, y la asociación abre una fuente de ingresos adicional con la venta a no socios. Es la fase donde la inversión empieza a generar retorno directo.

**Alcance:**

- **Módulo completo de gestión de eventos integrado al panel de administración**: creación, modificación y cierre de eventos desde un solo lugar — AMEXCAP deja de depender de proveedores externos para cada evento y toma control total de su propio calendario.
- **Sistema de registro y venta de boletos vinculado a la identidad de socio** — el socio compra con la misma cuenta que ya usa para todo lo demás, y el sistema reconoce automáticamente sus privilegios y precios, sin que tenga que demostrar nada.
- **Venta de boletos para no socios**, con un flujo público de checkout — los eventos abren su alcance más allá de la membresía y AMEXCAP estrena una nueva fuente de ingresos sin esfuerzo operativo adicional.
- **Boletos digitales con integración a Apple Wallet y Google Wallet** — el socio guarda su boleto en el celular como cualquier pase de avión o entrada de concierto, y el día del evento entra con un escaneo, sin filas ni listas impresas.
- **Notificaciones y recordatorios automáticos** de eventos vía WhatsApp y correo — invitaciones, confirmaciones y recordatorios salen solos, en lugar de tener al equipo persiguiendo a los socios uno por uno.
- **Biblioteca digital de publicaciones y estudios con control de acceso**: los socios acceden con su cuenta, los no socios pueden comprar publicaciones individuales — los estudios que AMEXCAP produce dejan de vivir solo en bandejas de entrada y se convierten en un activo siempre disponible y monetizable.
- **Historial de participación por socio** (a qué eventos asistió, qué publicaciones descargó) — AMEXCAP empieza por primera vez a entender quiénes son sus socios más activos y qué les interesa, abriendo la puerta a decisiones basadas en datos.

**Resultado esperado:** sobre la plataforma ya unificada, AMEXCAP construye un sistema de eventos propio que elimina proveedores externos, integra la identidad de socio en cada compra, abre una nueva fuente de ingresos con la venta a no socios, y profesionaliza por completo la experiencia del día del evento.

**Duración estimada:** 0.5 a 1 mes

---

# 5. Módulos opcionales (fases futuras)

Los siguientes módulos no forman parte del alcance principal, pero quedan habilitados gracias a los cimientos construidos y representan oportunidades naturales de expansión cuando la plataforma esté en marcha.

### 5.1 Estrategia de distribución de contenido y redes sociales

Publicación automatizada y recurrente de contenido de AMEXCAP en LinkedIn, X y otros canales, potenciada por inteligencia artificial — para que los estudios de la asociación dejen de ser un secreto bien guardado y empiecen a posicionar a AMEXCAP como referente informativo a nivel global.

### 5.2 Analítica de engagement de socios

Tableros que muestren qué socios leen estudios, abren boletines, asisten a eventos y participan activamente — para identificar a los socios más comprometidos, detectar a los que están en riesgo de no renovar, y enfocar los esfuerzos de retención donde más importan.

### 5.3 CRM y automatización de comunicaciones

Capa avanzada de comunicaciones con segmentación de audiencias, programación de envíos, plantillas reutilizables y métricas de apertura y clics — para convertir la operación de comunicación de AMEXCAP en una máquina de marketing profesional.

---

# 6. Cronograma e inversión estimada

| Fase | Alcance | Duración estimada | Inversión |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Fase 1 | Cimientos de la plataforma: backend, autenticación, paneles de socio y admin, automatización de renovaciones, sistema seguro de documentos, notificaciones multicanal | 2-3 meses | $350,000 MXN |
| Fase 2 | Reemplazo del sitio web y migración completa | 0.5-1 mes | $200,000 MXN |
| Fase 3 | Sistema de eventos: gestión, venta de boletos a socios y no socios, integración con wallet | 0.5-1 mes | $150,000 MXN |
| **Total** |  | **3-5 meses** | **$700,000 MXN** |

*El modelo por fases permite que cada etapa entregue valor de forma independiente. No es necesario comprometerse con las tres fases desde el inicio — cada fase se puede aprobar y ejecutar por separado.*

---

# 7. Siguientes pasos

1. **Reunión de revisión de propuesta** — agendar segunda llamada con José para revisar este documento, resolver dudas y definir prioridades.
2. **Definición de alcance detallado** — tras la aprobación conceptual, PRAGMA realiza un sprint de descubrimiento para detallar requerimientos, flujos y estimaciones precisas de la Fase 1.
3. **Propuesta económica final** — con el alcance detallado definido, se presenta la cotización formal de la primera fase.
4. **Arranque de proyecto** — kickoff, plan de trabajo y primeras entregas.

---

*Propuesta preparada por PRAGMA para AMEXCAP.*

*Contacto: Santiago Coronado*

*Fecha: abril 2026*

AMEXCAP es la voz de los fondos de inversión en México, con 130 socios entre fondos de capital de riesgo, crédito privado, crecimiento, infraestructura, energía y despachos especializados. Su operación, sin embargo, todavía depende de procesos manuales que consumen tiempo del equipo y limitan la experiencia que vive el socio.

PRAGMA propone construir una **plataforma integral** que centralice la gestión de membresías, la comunicación con socios, los eventos y el sitio web, todo bajo una sola identidad de socio y un solo panel de administración. El proyecto se entrega en tres fases secuenciales para que AMEXCAP reciba funcionalidad útil desde el primer mes y pueda distribuir la inversión en el tiempo, financiando cada fase conforme entrega valor en lugar de absorber el costo total de un solo golpe.

---

# 2. Situación actual

Durante la llamada de descubrimiento se identificaron los siguientes retos operativos:

1. **Renovación de membresías manual:** cada año el equipo contacta uno por uno a los 130 socios por correo, valida manualmente el tamaño de cada firma, reasigna la cuota correspondiente, envía la documentación y genera facturas a mano.
2. **Portal de socios abandonado:** el número de membresía que se le asigna a cada socio rara vez se usa; la mayoría lo pierde u olvida, y el equipo termina enviando todo por correo de cualquier manera.
3. **Registro de eventos desconectado:** para cada evento se contratan proveedores externos que construyen sitio y tienda de boletos desde cero, sin integración con la membresía.
4. **Eventos cerrados difíciles de coordinar:** la única forma de invitar a socios es por correo, WhatsApp y llamadas, persiguiéndolos uno por uno.
5. **Baja respuesta en la recolección de datos:** los Excel trimestrales que se envían a los fondos casi no se contestan porque los fondos no confían en cómo se manejará su información.
6. **Sitio web desactualizado:** el sitio actual no refleja el nivel de la asociación ni se integra con el resto de la operación.

---

La mayoría de estos retos son síntomas de un mismo problema de fondo: **AMEXCAP cuenta con sistemas, pero ninguno es efectivo ni cubre todas las funcionalidades que la asociación necesita.** Algunos están abandonados, otros funcionan parcialmente, y ninguno habla con los demás. # Turso Database Setup for Development & Production

This guide sets up separate Turso databases for development and production, with easy switching between local SQLite and Turso.

## Why Turso?

- **Managed cloud database** — No local files to manage
- **Closer to production** — Uses the same infrastructure as prod
- **Better for collaboration** — Share a dev database with teammates
- **Built for Vercel** — Native integration with Vercel deployments
- **Free tier available** — Generous free tier for development

## Installation

### 1. Install Turso CLI

```bash
# macOS
brew install tursodatabase/tap/turso

# Linux / other platforms
curl -sSfL https://get.tur.so/install.sh | bash
```

Verify:
```bash
turso --version
```

### 2. Create Turso Account

```bash
turso auth signup
# Follow the browser auth flow
```

### 3. Create Development & Production Databases

```bash
# Development database
turso db create pragma-web-dev

# Production database (when ready to deploy)
turso db create pragma-web-prod
```

### 4. Get Connection Strings

```bash
# Development
turso db shell pragma-web-dev .dump > /dev/null  # Test connection
turso db show pragma-web-dev --json

# Production
turso db show pragma-web-prod --json
```

Copy the URLs and auth tokens from the output.

### 5. Set Environment Variables

Create or update `.env.local`:

```bash
# Use local SQLite for development (default)
DATABASE_URL="file:local.db"
DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN=""

# Or switch to Turso development database:
# DATABASE_URL="libsql://pragma-web-dev-[username].turso.io"
# DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN="[your-token]"

# Production (Vercel will use these via dashboard)
# NEXT_PUBLIC_DATABASE_URL="libsql://pragma-web-prod-[username].turso.io"
# DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN="[your-prod-token]"
```

## Usage

### Local Development (Default)

```bash
npm run dev
# Uses local.db (SQLite)
```

### Switch to Turso Development

Edit `.env.local`:
```bash
DATABASE_URL="libsql://pragma-web-dev-[username].turso.io"
DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN="[your-dev-token]"
```

Then:
```bash
npm run dev
# Now using Turso development database
```

### Migrations

Migrations run automatically on startup via `initDb()` in `src/shared/lib/db.ts`. Both SQLite and Turso use the same migration code.

### Database Shell Access

```bash
# Connect to development database
turso db shell pragma-web-dev

# SQL commands
> SELECT * FROM quotes;
> .tables
> .exit
```

### Backups

```bash
# Export development database
turso db shell pragma-web-dev .dump > backup-dev.sql

# Export production database
turso db shell pragma-web-prod .dump > backup-prod.sql
```

## Production Deployment

### 1. Set Vercel Environment Variables

```bash
vercel env add DATABASE_URL
# libsql://pragma-web-prod-[username].turso.io

vercel env add DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN
# [your-prod-token]
```

### 2. Deploy

```bash
git push
vercel deploy
# Uses production database automatically
```

## Troubleshooting

**"Connection refused"**
- Check DATABASE_URL is correct
- Check DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN is set
- Run `turso db show pragma-web-dev` to verify database exists

**"Table already exists"**
- Normal on first run; migrations handle this gracefully
- SQLite and Turso use the same try/catch pattern

**Want to reset?**

```bash
# Drop and recreate Turso database
turso db destroy pragma-web-dev
turso db create pragma-web-dev

# Reset local SQLite
rm local.db
npm run dev  # Recreates and migrates
```

## Switching Between Local and Turso

| Scenario | DATABASE_URL | DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN |
|----------|---|---|
| **Local dev** | `file:local.db` | `` (empty) |
| **Turso dev** | `libsql://[db]-[user].turso.io` | `[token]` |
| **Turso prod** | `libsql://[db]-[user].turso.io` | `[token]` |

## Costs

- **Turso free tier**: 3 databases, 10GB storage, unlimited API calls
- **Turso paid**: $29/month per additional database

For a project like PRAGMA, the free tier is more than sufficient for development.

## Resources

- [Turso Documentation](https://docs.turso.tech)
- [Turso CLI Reference](https://docs.turso.tech/reference/cli)
- [Vercel + Turso Integration](https://vercel.com/docs/storage/vercel-postgres)

- Run `turso db show pragma-web-dev` to verify database exists

**"Table already exists"**
- Normal on first run; migrations handle this gracefully
- SQLite and Turso use the same try/catch pattern

**Want to reset?**

```bash
# Drop and recreate Turso database
turso db destroy pragma-web-dev
turso db create pragma-web-dev

# Reset local SQLite
rm local.db
npm run dev  # Recreates and migrates
```

## Switching Between Local and Turso

| Scenario | DATABASE_URL | DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN |
|----------|---|---|
| **Local dev** | `file:local.db` | `` (empty) |
| **Turso dev** | `libsql://[db]-[user].turso.io` | `[token]` |
| **Turso prod** | `libsql://[db]-[user].turso.io` | `[token]` |

## Costs

- **Turso free tier**: 3 databases, 10GB storage, unlimited API calls
- **Turso paid**: $29/month per additional database

For a project like PRAGMA, the free tier is more than sufficient for development.

## Resources

- [Turso Documentation](https://docs.turso.tech)
- [Turso CLI Reference](https://docs.turso.tech/reference/cli)
- [Vercel + Turso Integration](https://vercel.com/docs/storage/vercel-postgres)

#!/bin/bash

# Switch between local SQLite and Turso databases

if [ "$1" = "local" ]; then
    echo "Switching to local SQLite..."
    cat > .env.local.tmp <<EOF
# Local development database
DATABASE_URL="file:local.db"
DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN=""
EOF
    # Preserve other env vars
    grep -v "^DATABASE_URL\|^DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN" .env.local >> .env.local.tmp 2>/dev/null || true
    mv .env.local.tmp .env.local
    echo "✓ Switched to local SQLite (local.db)"

elif [ "$1" = "turso" ]; then
    if [ -z "$2" ]; then
        echo "Usage: npm run switch-db turso <url> <token>"
        echo ""
        echo "Example:"
        echo "  npm run switch-db turso libsql://db-user.turso.io abc123..."
        exit 1
    fi

    echo "Switching to Turso..."
    cat > .env.local.tmp <<EOF
# Turso development database
DATABASE_URL="$2"
DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN="$3"
EOF
    # Preserve other env vars
    grep -v "^DATABASE_URL\|^DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN" .env.local >> .env.local.tmp 2>/dev/null || true
    mv .env.local.tmp .env.local
    echo "✓ Switched to Turso"

elif [ "$1" = "status" ]; then
    echo "Current database configuration:"
    grep "^DATABASE_URL" .env.local || echo "  DATABASE_URL: not set"
    if grep -q "^DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN=" .env.local && [ -n "$(grep '^DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN=' .env.local | cut -d= -f2)" ]; then
        echo "  DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN: [set]"
    else
        echo "  DATABASE_AUTH_TOKEN: [not set]"
    fi

else
    echo "Usage: npm run switch-db [local|turso|status]"
    echo ""
    echo "Commands:"
    echo "  local              Switch to local SQLite (local.db)"
    echo "  turso <url> <token> Switch to Turso database"
    echo "  status             Show current database configuration"
    exit 1
fi
